title: Get started
text: SEMs ubiquitous social sciences, psychology, ecology, fields. INLAvaan package provides user-friendly interface fitting Bayesian SEMs using Integrated Nested Laplace Approximations (INLA, Rue, Martino, Chopin 2009). vignette guide basics using INLAvaan fit simple SEM. begin make sure installed INLAvaan package GitHub running commands .
code: # Load all libraries for this example library(INLAvaan) library(tidyverse) library(lavaan)

title: Get started
text: motivate use SEMs, consider introductory example Song Lee (2012): poorer glycemic control lead greater severity kidney disease? observe three indicators glycemic control (y1y_1, y2y_2, y3y_3) three indicators kidney disease severity (y4y_4, y5y_5, y6y_6).  Rather fitting separate regression models indicator, SEM allows us model relationship latent constructs , providing clearer coherent representation underlying processes. hypothesised SEM illustrated figure :

title: Get started
text: two-factor SEM described , easy simulate data using lavaan package . code : code , note true values parameters, including factor loadings Λ\Lambda, regression coefficient β\beta two latent variables, well residual latent variances Θ\Theta Ψ\Psi respectively.

title: Get started
text: Now simulated data, can fit SEM using INLAvaan. model syntax similar lavaan, making easy specify model. details model syntax, refer lavaan website. INLAvaan provides mirror functions main model fitting functions lavaan: acfa() mirrors lavaan::cfa() confirmatory factor analysis; asem() mirrors lavaan::sem() structural equation models; agrowth() mirrors lavaan::growth() latent growth curve models. code fit SEM model : INLAvaan computes approximation posterior density way Laplace approximation (Tierney, Kass, Kadane 1989). joint mode Hessian needs computed, gives Gaussian distribution joint posterior parameters. default method optimisation stats::nlminb(), optimisers can used specifying optim_method = "ucminf" ucminf package optim_method = "optim" call stats::optim() function method "BFGS". , marginal posterior distributions parameter can obtained one several ways, including 1) Skew normal fitting (marginal_method = "skewnorm", default method, see Chiuchiolo, Van Niekerk, Rue 2023); 2) Two-piece asymmetric Gaussian fitting (marginal_method = "asymgaus", see Martins et al. 2013); 3) Direct marginalisation joint Gaussian posterior (marginal_method = "marggaus"); 4) Sampling joint Gaussian posterior (marginal_method = "sampling"). marginal posterior distributions obtained, can use compute derived quantities interest via copula sampling. posterior predictive p-values (Gelman, Meng, Stern 1996) Deviance Information Criterion (DIC, Spiegelhalter et al. 2002) computed way. Often, posterior sampling takes longer model fitting , number samples can controlled via nsamp argument (default nsamp = 1000) can skipped altoghether (test = "none").

title: Get started
text: INLAvaan package uses prior specification syntax blavaan (Merkle Rosseel 2018; Merkle et al. 2021), detailed . Essentially, two ways set priors model parameters: 1) Globally parameters certain type (e.g., factor loadings, regression coefficients, etc.); 2) Individually specific parameters model syntax. default global priors derived blavaan: Note , INLAvaan uses separation strategy variance matrices, consequently places priors correlations instead covariances. , instead wished set global priors, say gamma distribution variances instead standard deviations (default), following: set individual priors specific parameters, can model syntax . instance, set normal prior mean 1 standard deviation 3 factor loading y3 eta1, normal prior mean 0 standard deviation 0.5 regression coefficient eta1 eta2, specify model follows:
code: priors_for()  # similar to blavaan::dpriors() #>                nu             alpha            lambda              beta  #>    "normal(0,32)"    "normal(0,10)"    "normal(0,10)"    "normal(0,10)"  #>             theta               psi               rho               tau  #> "gamma(1,.5)[sd]" "gamma(1,.5)[sd]"       "beta(1,1)"   "normal(0,1.5)" DP <- priors_for(theta = "gamma(1,1)", psi = "gamma(1,1)") DP #>              nu           alpha          lambda            beta           theta  #>  "normal(0,32)"  "normal(0,10)"  "normal(0,10)"  "normal(0,10)"    "gamma(1,1)"  #>             psi             rho             tau  #>    "gamma(1,1)"     "beta(1,1)" "normal(0,1.5)" ## fit <- asem(mod, dat, dpriors = DP)  # not run mod <- "   eta1 =~ y1 + y2 + prior('normal(1,3)')*y3   eta2 =~ y4 + y5 + y6   eta2 ~ prior('normal(0,.5)')*eta1 " ## fit <- asem(mod, dat)  # not run

text: Dependency R-INLA temporarily removed tThe current version INLAvaan (v0.2-0). wide class LVMs SEMs latent variables unstructured independent, current implementation sufficient. However, future versions INLAvaan re-introduce dependency R-INLA allow complex latent structures, spatial temporal dependencies.

title: Bayesian Fit Indices
text: Classical SEM fit indices (RMSEA, CFI, TLI, etc.) computed single maximum-likelihood chi-square statistic. Bayesian SEM, parameters random, posterior draw yields different chi-square value , therefore, different set fit indices. INLAvaan provides Bayesian analogues common indices, following framework Garnier-Villarreal Jorgensen (2020) (implemented blavaan). vignette covers: Mathematical definitions Bayesian fit indices. two rescaling methods ("devM" "MCMC"). worked example Holzinger–Swineford data. Comparison baseline model (incremental indices). Differences INLAvaan blavaan.

title: Bayesian Fit Indices
text: Let 𝛉(s)\boldsymbol\theta^{(s)} denote ss-th posterior draw model parameters (s=1,…,Ss = 1, \dots, S). per-sample deviance chi-square χs2=2[ℓsat−ℓ(𝛉(s))],   \chi^2_s = 2 \bigl[\ell_{\text{sat}} - \ell(\boldsymbol\theta^{(s)})\bigr],  ℓsat\ell_{\text{sat}} log-likelihood saturated model (sample moments equal model moments) ℓ(𝛉(s))\ell(\boldsymbol\theta^{(s)}) log-likelihood evaluated ss-th draw. equals NFML(𝛉(s))N \, F_{\text{ML}}(\boldsymbol\theta^{(s)}), FMLF_{\text{ML}} ML discrepancy function. INLAvaan supports two rescaling methods, controlled rescale argument: "devM" (default). Uses DIC-based effective number parameters pDp_D. ds=χs2−pD,df=p−pD,Nadj=N.   d_s = \chi^2_s - p_D, \qquad   \mathrm{df} = p - p_D, \qquad   N_{\mathrm{adj}} = N.  pDp_D unreasonable (pD≤0p_D \leq 0 pD≥pp_D \geq p), INLAvaan falls back using pD=qp_D = q (number free parameters). "MCMC". Uses classical chi-square N−1N - 1 scaling. ds=N−1Nχs2,df=p−q,Nadj=N−G,   d_s = \frac{N - 1}{N} \chi^2_s, \qquad   \mathrm{df} = p - q, \qquad   N_{\mathrm{adj}} = N - G,  qq number free parameters GG number groups. cases per-sample noncentrality parameter λ̂s=max(ds−df,0)\hat\lambda_s = \max(d_s - \mathrm{df},\, 0). following indices computed posterior draw ss, generating posterior distribution. BRMSEA (Bayesian RMSEA). BRMSEAs=λ̂sdf⋅Nadj⋅G.   \text{BRMSEA}_s = \sqrt{\frac{\hat\lambda_s}{\mathrm{df} \cdot N_\text{adj}}} \cdot \sqrt{G}. BGammaHat. BGammaHats=vv+2λ̂s/Nadj,   \text{BGammaHat}_s = \frac{v}{v + 2\hat\lambda_s / N_\text{adj}},  v=∑gpgv = \sum_g p_g total number observed variables across groups. Adjusted BGammaHat. adjBGammaHats=1−pdf(1−BGammaHats).   \text{adjBGammaHat}_s = 1 - \frac{p}{\mathrm{df}} \bigl(1 - \text{BGammaHat}_s\bigr). BMc (McDonald’s centrality index). BMcs=exp(−12λ̂s/Nadj).   \text{BMc}_s = \exp\!\bigl(-\tfrac{1}{2}\hat\lambda_s / N_\text{adj}\bigr). Incremental indices compare target model baseline (null) model. Let ds(0)d_s^{(0)}, df(0)\mathrm{df}^{(0)}, λ̂s(0)\hat\lambda_s^{(0)} denote corresponding quantities baseline model. BCFI (Bayesian CFI). BCFIs=1−λ̂sλ̂s(0).   \text{BCFI}_s = 1 - \frac{\hat\lambda_s}{\hat\lambda_s^{(0)}}. BTLI (Bayesian TLI). BTLIs=ds(0)/df(0)−ds/dfds(0)/df(0)−1.   \text{BTLI}_s = \frac{d_s^{(0)} / \mathrm{df}^{(0)} - d_s / \mathrm{df}}{d_s^{(0)} / \mathrm{df}^{(0)} - 1}. BNFI (Bayesian NFI). BNFIs=ds(0)−dsds(0).   \text{BNFI}_s = \frac{d_s^{(0)} - d_s}{d_s^{(0)}}. posterior expectations (EAP), standard deviations, quantile-based credible intervals, modes distributions reported summary() method.

title: Bayesian Fit Indices
text: fit three-factor CFA Holzinger–Swineford (1939) data. fitMeasures() returns posterior mean (EAP) Bayesian fit index alongside marginal log-likelihood, ppp, DIC, gradient diagnostics. inspect full posterior distribution index, use bfit_indices(). returns S3 object class "bfit_indices". Calling summary() provides table posterior summaries (Mean, SD, 2.5%, 50%, 97.5%, Mode) index. can also access raw per-sample vectors custom analysis:  Posterior distribution BRMSEA
code: HS.model <- "   visual  =~ x1 + x2 + x3   textual =~ x4 + x5 + x6   speed   =~ x7 + x8 + x9 "  fit <- acfa(HS.model, HolzingerSwineford1939, verbose = FALSE) fitMeasures(fit) #>         npar   margloglik          ppp          dic        p_dic       BRMSEA  #>           21    -3823.329        0.000     7520.677       22.472        0.097  #>    BGammaHat adjBGammaHat          BMc     grad_inf grad_inf_rel      grad_l2  #>        0.955        0.911        0.900     2.35e-03     2.38e-02     4.41e-03 bfi <- bfit_indices(fit) bfi #> Posterior summary of devM-based Bayesian fit indices (nsamp = 500):  #>  #>       BRMSEA    BGammaHat adjBGammaHat          BMc  #>        0.097        0.955        0.911        0.900 summary(bfi) #>  #> Posterior summary of devM-based Bayesian fit indices (nsamp = 500): #>  #>               Mean    SD X2.5.  X50. X97.5.  Mode #> BRMSEA       0.097 0.006 0.087 0.096  0.109 0.094 #> BGammaHat    0.955 0.005 0.944 0.956  0.963 0.958 #> adjBGammaHat 0.911 0.010 0.889 0.912  0.927 0.915 #> BMc          0.900 0.011 0.876 0.901  0.918 0.905 hist(bfi$indices$BRMSEA, breaks = 30, main = "BRMSEA", xlab = "BRMSEA",      col = "steelblue", border = "white")

title: Bayesian Fit Indices
text: Incremental indices (BCFI, BTLI, BNFI) require baseline model. common choice independence (uncorrelated) model. Now pass baseline model fitMeasures() bfit_indices():
code: null.model <- "   x1 ~~ x1   x2 ~~ x2   x3 ~~ x3   x4 ~~ x4   x5 ~~ x5   x6 ~~ x6   x7 ~~ x7   x8 ~~ x8   x9 ~~ x9 "  fit_null <- acfa(null.model, HolzingerSwineford1939, verbose = FALSE) fitMeasures(fit, baseline.model = fit_null) #>         npar   margloglik          ppp          dic        p_dic       BRMSEA  #>           21    -3823.329        0.000     7520.677       22.472        0.096  #>    BGammaHat adjBGammaHat          BMc         BCFI         BTLI         BNFI  #>        0.955        0.911        0.900        0.928        0.886        0.907  #>     grad_inf grad_inf_rel      grad_l2  #>     2.35e-03     2.38e-02     4.41e-03 bfi_inc <- bfit_indices(fit, baseline.model = fit_null) summary(bfi_inc) #>  #> Posterior summary of devM-based Bayesian fit indices (nsamp = 500): #>  #>               Mean    SD X2.5.  X50. X97.5.  Mode #> BRMSEA       0.096 0.006 0.087 0.095  0.107 0.094 #> BGammaHat    0.956 0.005 0.945 0.956  0.964 0.958 #> adjBGammaHat 0.912 0.010 0.891 0.913  0.928 0.915 #> BMc          0.901 0.011 0.878 0.902  0.919 0.905 #> BCFI         0.929 0.008 0.912 0.930  0.943 0.932 #> BTLI         0.887 0.013 0.860 0.889  0.909 0.892 #> BNFI         0.907 0.008 0.891 0.908  0.920 0.910

title: Bayesian Fit Indices
text: rescale argument controls chi-square degrees freedom computed. default "devM", subtracts DIC-based pDp_D deviance. use classical N−1N - 1 scaling instead, set rescale = "MCMC": two methods generally produce different results, especially informative priors pDp_D deviates substantially qq.
code: bfi_mcmc <- bfit_indices(fit, rescale = "MCMC") summary(bfi_mcmc) #>  #> Posterior summary of MCMC-based Bayesian fit indices (nsamp = 500): #>  #>               Mean    SD X2.5.  X50. X97.5.  Mode #> BRMSEA       0.108 0.005 0.100 0.107  0.122 0.108 #> BGammaHat    0.941 0.006 0.927 0.942  0.949 0.942 #> adjBGammaHat 0.890 0.010 0.863 0.891  0.905 0.891 #> BMc          0.869 0.012 0.837 0.871  0.887 0.871

title: Bayesian Fit Indices
text: INLAvaan’s Bayesian fit indices follow mathematical framework blavaan (Merkle et al. 2021), implementation differences: Currently, INLAvaan supports pDp_D DIC (.e., p_dic). "ppmc" rescaling method (computes replicated data posterior predictive) yet available.

title: Mediation Analysis
text: verify INLAvaan recovers correct parameters, simulate data “truth” known. logic follows: Generate… XX normally; MM dependent XX coefficient 0.5; YY dependent MM coefficient 0.7. Critically, add XX generation YY. means true direct effect (cc) 0, relationship fully mediated. expect model estimate ≈0.5a \approx 0.5, b≈0.7b \approx 0.7, indirect effect ab≈0.35ab \approx 0.35. direct effect cc close zero.
code: set.seed(11) n <- 100  # sample size  # 1. Predictor X <- rnorm(n)  # 2. Mediator (Path a = 0.5) M <- 0.5 * X + rnorm(n)  # 3. Outcome (Path b = 0.7, Path c = 0) Y <- 0.7 * M + rnorm(n)   dat <- data.frame(X = X, Y = Y, M = M)

title: Mediation Analysis
text: standard lavaan syntax mediation model straightforward (note use := operator define indirect effect new parameter.): model fit using asem(). meanstructure = TRUE argument supplied estimate intercepts variables. user may wish specify different prior distributions parameters. See relevant section Get started vignetted details.
code: mod <- "   # Direct effect (path c)   Y ~ c*X    # Mediator paths (path a and b)   M ~ a*X   Y ~ b*M    # Define Indirect effect (a*b)   ab := a*b    # Define Total effect   total := c + (a*b) " library(INLAvaan) fit <- asem(mod, dat, meanstructure = TRUE) #> ℹ Finding posterior mode. #> ✔ Finding posterior mode. [33ms] #>  #> ℹ Computing the Hessian. #> ✔ Computing the Hessian. [62ms] #>  #> ℹ Performing VB correction. #> ✔ VB correction; mean |δ| = 0.012σ. [161ms] #>  #> ⠙ Fitting 0/7 skew-normal marginals. #> ✔ Fitting 7/7 skew-normal marginals. [112ms] #>  #> ℹ Adjusting copula correlations (NORTA). #> ✔ Adjusting copula correlations (NORTA). [23ms] #>  #> ⠙ Posterior sampling and summarising. #> ✔ Posterior sampling and summarising. [291ms] #>

title: Mediation Analysis
text: summary output provides posterior mean, standard deviation, 95% credible intervals paths. Looking Regressions Defined Parameters sections output: intercepts non-significant, since simulated data true means zero. Path aa (M ~ X) estimated 0.526 (true value 0.5). Path bb (Y ~ M) estimated 0.772 (true value 0.7). Path cc (Y ~ X) estimated -0.059. 95% Credible Interval [-0.290, 0.172] includes zero, correctly identifying direct effect. Indirect Effect abab estimated 0.403 (true value 0.35). interval [0.209, 0.598] cross zero, indicating significant mediation. sum direct indirect effects (c+abc + ab). tells us 1-unit increase XX leads total increase roughly 0.351 YY. Note: simulation, even though direct effect non-significant (close zero), total effect significant mechanism via MM strong. illustrates “full mediation” scenario: XX affects YY, MM.

title: Missing Values
code: library(INLAvaan) mod <- "     # Latent variable definitions     ind60 =~ x1 + x2 + x3     dem60 =~ y1 + y2 + y3 + y4     dem65 =~ y5 + y6 + y7 + y8      # Latent regressions     dem60 ~ ind60     dem65 ~ ind60 + dem60      # Residual correlations     y1 ~~ y5     y2 ~~ y4 + y6     y3 ~~ y7     y4 ~~ y8     y6 ~~ y8   " dat <- lavaan::PoliticalDemocracy  # Simulate missingness (MCAR) set.seed(221) mis <- matrix(rbinom(prod(dim(dat)), 1, 0.99), nrow(dat), ncol(dat)) datmiss <- dat * mis datmiss[datmiss == 0] <- NA

title: Multilevel SEM
text: use Demo.twolevel dataset included R package lavaan, make easier follow, interpret variables within educational context. data contains following information: Clusters (cluster): 200 different schools. Observations: 2500 students nested within schools. Outcomes (y1, y2, y3): Three distinct survey items measuring “Academic Performance.” Within-level Predictors (x1, x2, x3): Student-specific factors Study Hours, Sleep Hours, Attendance. -level Predictors (w1, w2): School-level factors Teacher Experience, School Budget. model, assume two latent variables: fw measuring individual students aptitude (.e. student ability) fb measuring school quality, .e. shared variance performance attributable school environment. envisaged two-level SEM can visualized follows:
code: graph LR      %% --- Level 1: Within (Student) ---     subgraph L1 [Level 1: Within-Student]         direction LR         x1[x1] & x2[x2] & x3[x3] --> fw((fw))         fw --> y1_w[y1] & y2_w[y2] & y3_w[y3]     end      %% --- Separator (Invisible edge to force stacking if needed) ---     L1 ~~~ L2      %% --- Level 2: Between (School) ---     subgraph L2 [Level 2: Between-School]         direction LR         w1[w1] & w2[w2] --> fb((fb))         fb --> y1_b[y1] & y2_b[y2] & y3_b[y3]     end          %% --- Styling ---     classDef latent fill:#f9f9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,shape:circle;     classDef observed fill:#fff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,shape:rect;      class fw,fb latent;     class x1,x2,x3,w1,w2,y1_b,y2_b,y3_b,y1_w,y2_w,y3_w observed;

title: Multilevel SEM
text: First, load INLAvaan dataset.

title: Multilevel SEM
text: INLAvaan (following lavaan syntax), specify model level using level: <block> keywords. example, Level 1: define latent variable fw (Student Ability) regress individual predictors (x). Level 2: define latent variable fb (School Quality) regress school-level predictors (w). use asem() function (Approximate SEM) fit model. Crucially, must specify cluster argument identify grouping variable.
code: mod <- "   level: 1       # Measurement model (Within-student)       fw =~ y1 + y2 + y3       # Structural model: Individual predictors       fw ~ x1 + x2 + x3    level: 2       # Measurement model (Between-school)       fb =~ y1 + y2 + y3       # Structural model: School-level predictors       fb ~ w1 + w2 " fit <- asem(mod, data = Demo.twolevel, cluster = "cluster") #> ℹ Finding posterior mode. #> ✔ Finding posterior mode. title: Sampling from the Generative Model
text: fitting Bayesian SEM INLAvaan, often want draw samples model —either using posterior prior parameter values. sampling() function exactly : propagates parameter draws full generative chain 𝛉⏟parameters→𝛈⏟latent variables→𝐲*⏟observed variables \underbrace{\boldsymbol\theta}_{\text{parameters}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{\boldsymbol\eta}_{\text{latent variables}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{\mathbf{y}^*}_{\text{observed variables}} producing samples tied individual observation. distinct predict(), returns individual-specific factor scores 𝛈∣𝐲,𝛉\boldsymbol\eta \mid \mathbf{y}, \boldsymbol\theta. Typical use cases include posterior predictive checks (PPCs) prior predictive checks.

title: Sampling from the Generative Model
text: Let 𝛉(s)\boldsymbol\theta^{(s)} (s=1,…,Ss = 1, \dots, S) denote one parameter draw. draw SEM matrices 𝚲\boldsymbol\Lambda, 𝚿\boldsymbol\Psi, 𝐁\mathbf{B}, 𝛂\boldsymbol\alpha, 𝛎\boldsymbol\nu, 𝚯\boldsymbol\Theta constructed. generative chain : 1. Latent variables. 𝛈(s)∼𝒩((𝐈−𝐁)−1𝛂,𝚽),𝚽=(𝐈−𝐁)−1𝚿[(𝐈−𝐁)−1]′. \boldsymbol\eta^{(s)} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\bigl(   (\mathbf{} - \mathbf{B})^{-1}\boldsymbol\alpha,\;   \boldsymbol\Phi \bigr), \qquad \boldsymbol\Phi = (\mathbf{} - \mathbf{B})^{-1}\boldsymbol\Psi\,   [(\mathbf{} - \mathbf{B})^{-1}]'. 2. Observed variables. 𝐲*(s)∼𝒩(𝚲𝛈(s)+𝛎,𝚯). \mathbf{y}^{*(s)} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\bigl(   \boldsymbol\Lambda\,\boldsymbol\eta^{(s)} + \boldsymbol\nu,\;   \boldsymbol\Theta \bigr). prior = FALSE (default), 𝛉(s)\boldsymbol\theta^{(s)} comes posterior; prior = TRUE, parameter drawn independently prior.

title: Sampling from the Generative Model
text: default type "lavaan" returns S×pS \times p matrix lavaan-side (constrained) parameter draws:
code: dat <- lavaan::HolzingerSwineford1939 mod <- "   visual  =~ x1 + x2 + x3   textual =~ x4 + x5 + x6   speed   =~ x7 + x8 + x9 " fit <- acfa(mod, dat, verbose = FALSE) theta_post <- sampling(fit, type = "lavaan", nsamp = 2000) dim(theta_post) #> [1] 2000   21 head(theta_post[, 1:4]) #>      visual=~x2 visual=~x3 textual=~x5 textual=~x6 #> [1,]  0.4947745  0.9134944    1.064446   0.8717411 #> [2,]  0.6103204  0.6821216    1.099239   0.9374892 #> [3,]  0.5349267  0.6214083    1.115466   1.0028337 #> [4,]  0.5136245  0.6275826    1.071347   0.8962592 #> [5,]  0.5228936  0.6930980    1.079830   0.8992231 #> [6,]  0.5221700  0.6531219    1.187943   1.0811596 eta  <- sampling(fit, type = "latent",   nsamp = 1000) ystar <- sampling(fit, type = "observed", nsamp = 1000) dim(eta)    # 1000 x 3 (one per latent factor) #> [1] 1000    3 dim(ystar)  # 1000 x 9 (one per observed indicator) #> [1] 1000    9 all_samps <- sampling(fit, type = "all", nsamp = 1000) names(all_samps) #> [1] "lavaan"   "theta"    "latent"   "observed" "implied"

what: Comparing samples to the fitted marginals
title: Sampling from the Generative Model
text: INLAvaan approximates marginal posterior skew-normal density. can overlay sampling() histogram (drawn via copula method) top fitted density curve stored pdf_data verify agree. Figure 1: Copula samples (histogram) versus fitted skew-normal marginal (red curve) free parameters.
code: # 1. Draw copula samples samp_cop <- sampling(fit, type = "lavaan", nsamp = 1000, samp_copula = TRUE)  # 2. Retrieve the fitted skew-normal densities int <- fit@external$inlavaan_internal pdf_data <- int$pdf_data  # 3. Build a long data frame for ggplot par_names <- colnames(samp_cop) hist_df <- data.frame(   param = rep(par_names, each = nrow(samp_cop)),   value = as.vector(samp_cop) ) hist_df$param <- factor(hist_df$param, levels = par_names)  curve_df <- do.call(rbind, Map(function(nm, df) {   data.frame(param = nm, x = df$x, y = df$y) }, par_names, pdf_data[par_names])) curve_df$param <- factor(curve_df$param, levels = par_names)  # 4. Plot ggplot(hist_df, aes(x = value)) +   geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 50,                  fill = "grey40", alpha = 0.5) +   geom_line(data = curve_df, aes(x = x, y = y),             colour = "#00A6AA", linewidth = 0.8) +   facet_wrap(~param, scales = "free") +   labs(x = "Parameter value", y = "Density",        title = "Copula samples vs. fitted skew-normal posterior") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

title: Sampling from the Generative Model
text: default, sampling() uses copula method, respects skew-normal marginals. Setting samp_copula = FALSE uses multivariate Gaussian (Laplace) approximation instead. difference visible parameters asymmetric posteriors, variance components. Figure 2: Comparison marginal histograms copula sampling (blue) Gaussian sampling (orange) parameters. symmetric posteriors (e.g., factor loadings), two methods nearly identical. positively-skewed posteriors (e.g., residual variances), copula method captures asymmetry Gaussian approximation symmetric construction.
code: samp_gauss <- sampling(fit, type = "lavaan", nsamp = 5000, samp_copula = FALSE)  cop_df <- data.frame(   param = rep(par_names, each = nrow(samp_cop)),   value = as.vector(samp_cop),   method = "Copula" ) gauss_df <- data.frame(   param = rep(par_names, each = nrow(samp_gauss)),   value = as.vector(samp_gauss),   method = "Gaussian" ) both_df <- rbind(cop_df, gauss_df) both_df$param <- factor(both_df$param, levels = par_names) both_df$method <- factor(both_df$method, levels = c("Copula", "Gaussian"))  ggplot(both_df, aes(x = value, fill = method)) +   geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 50,                  alpha = 0.45, position = "identity") +   facet_wrap(~param, scales = "free") +   scale_fill_manual(values = c(Copula = "#00A6AA", Gaussian = "#F18F00")) +   labs(x = "Parameter value", y = "Density", fill = "Method",        title = "Copula vs. Gaussian posterior sampling") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +   theme(legend.position = "top")

title: Sampling from the Generative Model
text: Setting prior = TRUE draws parameters prior distribution (independently, ignoring data) propagates generative model. useful checking whether priors imply sensible ranges observed data. Figure 3: Model-implied distribution x1 prior (orange) posterior (blue).
code: y_prior <- sampling(fit, type = "observed", nsamp = 10000, prior = TRUE) #> Prior sampling: 6288 of 16288 draws (38.6%) rejected (non-PD model-implied #> covariance). y_post  <- sampling(fit, type = "observed", nsamp = 10000, prior = FALSE) df_pp <- data.frame(   x1 = c(y_prior[, "x1"], y_post[, "x1"]),   source = rep(c("Prior", "Posterior"), each = 2000) )  ggplot(df_pp, aes(x = x1, fill = source)) +   geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 50,                  alpha = 0.5, position = "identity") +   scale_fill_manual(values = c(Prior = "#F18F00", Posterior = "#00A6AA")) +   labs(x = "x1", y = "Density", fill = NULL,        title = "Prior vs. posterior predictive distribution of x1") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +   theme(legend.position = "top")

title: Approximate Bayesian Latent Variable Analysis
text: Efficient approximate Bayesian inference Structural Equation Models. Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods remain gold standard exact Bayesian inference, can prohibitively slow iterative model development. INLAvaan offers rapid alternative latent variable analysis, delivering Bayesian results (near) speed frequentist estimators. achieves custom, ground-implementation Integrated Nested Laplace Approximation (INLA), engineered specifically lavaan modelling framework.

title: Approximate Bayesian Latent Variable Analysis

what: INLAvaan: Approximate Bayesian Latent Variable Analysis — INLAvaan-package
title: INLAvaan: Approximate Bayesian Latent Variable Analysis — INLAvaan-package
text: Implements approximate Bayesian inference Structural Equation Models (SEM) using custom adaptation Integrated Nested Laplace Approximation described Rue et al. (2009) doi:10.1111/j.1467-9868.2008.00700.x . Provides computationally efficient alternative Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) Bayesian estimation, allowing users fit latent variable models using 'lavaan' syntax.

title: Fit an Approximate Bayesian Confirmatory Factor Analysis Model — acfa
text: model description user-specified model. Typically, model     described using lavaan model syntax. See     model.syntax information. Alternatively,     parameter table (eg. output lavParTable() function) also     accepted. data optional data frame containing observed variables used     model. variables declared ordered factors, lavaan     treat ordinal variables. dp Default prior distributions different types     parameters, typically result call dpriors().     See dpriors() help file information. test Character indicating whether compute posterior fit indices. Defaults "standard". Change "none" skip computations. vb_correction Logical indicating whether apply variational Bayes correction posterior mean vector estimates. Defaults TRUE. marginal_method method approximating marginal posterior distributions. Options include "skewnorm" (skew-normal), "asymgaus" (two-piece asymmetric Gaussian), "marggaus" (marginalising Laplace approximation), "sampling" (sampling joint Laplace approximation). marginal_correction type correction use fitting skew-normal two-piece Gaussian marginals. "hessian" computes full Hessian-based correction (slow), "shortcut" (default) computes diagonals, "none" (FALSE) applies correction. nsamp number samples draw sampling-based approaches (including posterior sampling model fit indices). samp_copula Logical. TRUE (default), posterior samples drawn using copula method fitted marginals (e.g. skew-normal asymmetric Gaussian), NORTA correlation adjustment. FALSE, samples drawn Gaussian (Laplace) approximation. re sn_fit_logthresh log-threshold fitting skew-normal. Points log-posterior drop threshold (relative maximum) excluded fit. Defaults -6. sn_fit_temp Temperature parameter fitting skew-normal. NA, temperature included optimisation skew normal fit. sn_fit_sample Logical. TRUE (default), parametric skew-normal fitted posterior samples covariance defined parameters. FALSE, summarised using kernel density estimation instead. control list control parameters optimiser. verbose Logical indicating whether print progress messages. debug Logical indicating whether return debug information. add_priors Logical indicating whether include prior densities posterior computation. optim_method optimisation method use finding posterior mode. Options include "nlminb" (default), "ucminf", "optim" (BFGS). numerical_grad Logical indicating whether use numerical gradients optimisation. Defaults FALSE use analytical gradients. cores Integer NULL. Number cores parallel marginal fitting. NULL (default), serial execution used unless number free parameters exceeds 120, case parallelisation enabled automatically using available physical cores. Set 1L force serial execution. cores > 1, marginal fits distributed across cores using parallel::mclapply() (fork-based; parallelism Windows). ... Additional arguments passed lavaan::lavaan model fitting function.

title: Bayesian Fit Indices — bfit_indices
text: object object class INLAvaan. baseline.model optional INLAvaan object representing baseline (null) model. Required incremental fit indices (BCFI, BTLI, BNFI). rescale Character string controlling Bayesian chi-square rescaled. "devM" (default) subtracts pD deviance sample. "MCMC" uses classical chi-square classical df sample. nsamp Number posterior samples draw. Defaults value used fitting model. samp_copula Logical. TRUE (default), posterior samples drawn using copula method fitted marginals. FALSE, samples drawn Gaussian (Laplace) approximation.

title: Bayesian Fit Indices — bfit_indices
text: S3 object class "bfit_indices" containing: indices Named list numeric vectors (one per posterior sample) computed fit index. details List chisq (per-sample deviance), df, pD, rescale, nsamp. Use summary() obtain table posterior summaries (Mean, SD, quantiles, Mode) index.

title: Compare Bayesian Models Fitted with INLAvaan — compare

title: Compare Bayesian Models Fitted with INLAvaan — compare
text: first argument x serves baseline (null) model. models (including baseline) appear comparison table. baseline also passed fitMeasures() incremental fit indices (BCFI, BTLI, BNFI) requested via fit.measures. default table always includes: npar: Number free parameters. Marg.Loglik: Approximated marginal log-likelihood. logBF: Natural-log Bayes factor relative best model. DIC / pD: Deviance Information Criterion effective number parameters (test != "none" used fitting). Set fit.measures character vector measure names (anything returned fitMeasures()) append extra columns. Use fit.measures = "" include every available measure.

title: Fit a skew normal distribution to log-density evaluations — fit_skew_normal
code: # Fit a SN curve to gamma log-density logdens <- function(x) dgamma(x, shape = 3, rate = 1, log = TRUE)  x_grid <- seq(0.1, 8, length.out = 21) y_log  <- sapply(x_grid, logdens) y_log  <- y_log - max(y_log)  # normalise to have maximum at zero  res <- fit_skew_normal(x_grid, y_log, temp = 10) unlist(res) #>           xi        omega        alpha         logC            k         rmse  #>  0.842445393  2.539226101  3.310505376  1.332422700 10.000000000  0.002109188  #>         nmad  #>  0.173298470   # Compare truth vs skew-normal approximation x_fine <- seq(0.1, 8, length.out = 200) y_true <- exp(logdens(x_fine)) y_sn   <- dsnorm(x_fine, xi = res$xi, omega = res$omega, alpha = res$alpha)  plot(x_fine, y_true, type = "n", xlab = "x", ylab = "Density", bty = "n") polygon(c(x_fine, rev(x_fine)), c(y_true, rep(0, length(x_fine))),         col = adjustcolor("#131516", 0.25), border = NA) lines(x_fine, y_sn, col = "#00A6AA", lwd = 2) legend("topright", legend = c("Truth", "SN Approx."),        fill = c(adjustcolor("#131516", 0.25), NA), border = NA,        col = c(NA, "#00A6AA"), lwd = c(NA, 2), lty = c(NA, 1),        bty = "n")

title: Fit an Approximate Bayesian Latent Variable Model — inlavaan
code: inlavaan(   model,   data,   model.type = "sem",   dp = priors_for(),   test = "standard",   vb_correction = TRUE,   marginal_method = c("skewnorm", "asymgaus", "marggaus", "sampling"),   marginal_correction = c("shortcut", "hessian", "none"),   nsamp = 500,   samp_copula = TRUE,   sn_fit_logthresh = -6,   sn_fit_temp = NA,   sn_fit_sample = TRUE,   control = list(),   verbose = TRUE,   debug = FALSE,   add_priors = TRUE,   optim_method = c("nlminb", "ucminf", "optim"),   numerical_grad = FALSE,   cores = NULL,   ... )

title: Fit an Approximate Bayesian Latent Variable Model — inlavaan
text: model description user-specified model. Typically, model     described using lavaan model syntax. See     model.syntax information. Alternatively,     parameter table (eg. output lavParTable() function) also     accepted. data optional data frame containing observed variables used     model. variables declared ordered factors, lavaan     treat ordinal variables. model.type Set model type: possible values     "cfa", "sem" "growth". may affect     starting values computed, may used alter terminology     used summary output, layout path diagrams     based fitted lavaan object. dp Default prior distributions different types     parameters, typically result call dpriors().     See dpriors() help file information. test Character indicating whether compute posterior fit indices. Defaults "standard". Change "none" skip computations. vb_correction Logical indicating whether apply variational Bayes correction posterior mean vector estimates. Defaults TRUE. marginal_method method approximating marginal posterior distributions. Options include "skewnorm" (skew-normal), "asymgaus" (two-piece asymmetric Gaussian), "marggaus" (marginalising Laplace approximation), "sampling" (sampling joint Laplace approximation). marginal_correction type correction use fitting skew-normal two-piece Gaussian marginals. "hessian" computes full Hessian-based correction (slow), "shortcut" (default) computes diagonals, "none" (FALSE) applies correction. nsamp number samples draw sampling-based approaches (including posterior sampling model fit indices). samp_copula Logical. TRUE (default), posterior samples drawn using copula method fitted marginals (e.g. skew-normal asymmetric Gaussian), NORTA correlation adjustment. FALSE, samples drawn Gaussian (Laplace) approximation. re sn_fit_logthresh log-threshold fitting skew-normal. Points log-posterior drop threshold (relative maximum) excluded fit. Defaults -6. sn_fit_temp Temperature parameter fitting skew-normal. NA, temperature included optimisation skew normal fit. sn_fit_sample Logical. TRUE (default), parametric skew-normal fitted posterior samples covariance defined parameters. FALSE, summarised using kernel density estimation instead. control list control parameters optimiser. verbose Logical indicating whether print progress messages. debug Logical indicating whether return debug information. add_priors Logical indicating whether include prior densities posterior computation. optim_method optimisation method use finding posterior mode. Options include "nlminb" (default), "ucminf", "optim" (BFGS). numerical_grad Logical indicating whether use numerical gradients optimisation. Defaults FALSE use analytical gradients. cores Integer NULL. Number cores parallel marginal fitting. NULL (default), serial execution used unless number free parameters exceeds 120, case parallelisation enabled automatically using available physical cores. Set 1L force serial execution. cores > 1, marginal fits distributed across cores using parallel::mclapply() (fork-based; parallelism Windows). ... Additional arguments passed lavaan::lavaan model fitting function.

title: Specify priors for a SEM — priors_for
text: function provides convenient way specify prior distributions different types parameters structural equation model (SEM). uses registry default priors common lavaan parameter types (e.g., loadings, regressions, residuals, etc.) allows users override defaults passing named arguments. parameter names, default settings, : nu = "normal(0,32)": Observed variable intercepts alpha = "normal(0,10)": Latent variable intercepts lambda = "normal(0,10)": Factor loadings beta = "normal(0,10)": Regression coefficients theta = "gamma(1,.5)[sd]": Residual precisions psi = "gamma(1,.5)[sd]": Latent variable precisions rho = "beta(1,1)": Correlations (latent observed) tau = "normal(0,1.5)": Thresholds ordinal variables Note normal distributions parameterised using standard deviations, variances. example, normal(0,10) means normal distribution mean 0 standard deviation 10 (variance 10).

title: Fast Approximation of Skew-Normal Quantile Function — qsnorm_fast
text: Vector quantiles.

title: Fast Approximation of Skew-Normal Quantile Function — qsnorm_fast
text: function implements high-performance approximation skew-normal quantile function based algorithm described Luu (2016). method uses domain decomposition strategy achieve high accuracy (\(< 10^{-7}\) relative error) without iterative numerical inversion. domain split two regions: Tail Regions: extreme probabilities \(\vert u \vert\) large, quantile approximated using Lambert W-function, \(W(z)\), solving \(z = \Phi(q)\) via asymptotic expansion: $$q \approx \sqrt{2 W\left(\frac{1}{2\pi (1-p)^2}\right)}$$ Central Region: main body distribution, function uses high-order Taylor expansion inverse error function around carefully selected expansion point $x_0$: $$\Phi^{-1}(p) \approx \sum_{k=0}^5 c_k (z - x_0)^k$$ approach significantly faster standard numerical inversion (e.g., uniroot) maintaining sufficient precision statistical applications.

title: Fast Approximation of Skew-Normal Quantile Function — qsnorm_fast
text: Luu, T. (2016). Fast accurate parallel computation quantile functions random number generation #' (Doctoral thesis). UCL (University College London). https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/1482128/

title: INLAvaan (development version)
text: Implement NORTA (NORmal Anything) correlation adjustment SN copula, posterior samples correct skew-normal marginals correct Pearson correlations. Copula sampling now default (samp_copula = TRUE). Use Cholesky factorisation precision matrix covariance log-determinant calculations, replacing raw solve(). Draw posterior samples store ; downstream consumers (covariances, defined parameters, ppp, DIC, predict(), standardisedsolution()) now reuse single draw. Use pre-computed Owen-scrambled Sobol sequence; fall back {qrng} larger sequences needed. QMC sample size now scales model dimension. Add vb_correction argument acfa(), asem(), agrowth(). Add params logscale options visual_debug. Small optimisations volume correction skew-normal marginalisation. ggplot2 now optional; plots work base R graphics. Fixed bug qsnorm_fast() incorrectly handled sign symmetries. NEW sampling() function produce draws posterior (prior) SEM generative model. NEW Bayesian Fit Indices included fitmeasures(); summary statistics available via bfit_indices(). NEW parallelism feature skew normal fit, automatically runs total available cores m>120m>120. NEW predict() now able generate predictions observed data. defined params, ’s possible fit skew normal approximation posterior marginal based samples calling sn_fit_sample = TRUE. Improved plot() method.

title: INLAvaan 0.2.3
text: CRAN release: 2026-01-28 Improved axis scanning, skewness correction, VB mean correction routine. Bug fixes CRAN. Updated README example.

title: INLAvaan 0.2.2
text: CRAN release: 2026-01-27 hood, use lavaan’s MVN log-likelihood function compute single- multi-level log-likelihoods. Added support multi-level SEM models. Added support binary data using PML estimator lavaan. NOTE: Ordinal possible theory, package still lacks proper prior support thresholds. Added support missing = "ML" handle FIML missing data.

title: INLAvaan 0.2.1
text: Support lavaan 0.6-21. Implemented variational Bayes mean correction posterior marginals. Defined parameters now available, e.g. mediation analysis. Prepare CRAN release.

title: INLAvaan 0.2
text: INLAvaan rewritten ground specifically SEM models. new version call R-INLA directly, instead uses core approximation ideas fit SEM models efficiently. Features restricted normal likelihoods continuous observations now. Support models lavaan/blavaan can fit, including CFA, SEM, growth curve models. Support multigroup analysis. Added PPP DIC model fit indices. Added prior specification model parameters. Added support fixed values parameter constraints. Initial CRAN submission.

title: INLAvaan 0.1
text: Used rgeneric functionality R-INLA implement basic SEM framework.
